resume:
Senior Clinical Operations Manager | Acute Care & Inpatient Services

Summary
Seasoned clinical operations leader with fourteen years of progressive responsibility across community hospitals, a regional academic medical center, and a multi-site integrated delivery network. I have owned inpatient throughput, emergency department flow, nursing staffing models, and regulatory readiness for Joint Commission and CMS Conditions of Participation surveys. My orientation is operational: I sit between bedside nursing leadership and the executive suite, translating capacity pressure into staffing decisions, and translating financial targets into bedside-feasible workflows. I am a registered nurse by training (BSN, MSN in Healthcare Administration) and have maintained an active license throughout my administrative career so that I never lose contact with the realities of patient care.

Most Recent Role
Mercy Regional Health System — Senior Clinical Operations Manager, Inpatient Division (2019 to present)
Report to the Chief Nursing Officer. Accountable for operations across four medical-surgical units, a thirty-two bed step-down unit, and the patient flow command center for a 480-bed tertiary facility. I directly manage six nurse managers and indirectly oversee roughly 420 nursing and support staff. My remit covers daily bed management, surge planning, agency and travel-nurse utilization, length-of-stay reduction initiatives, and the unit-level execution of system quality goals. During the pandemic surges I stood up and ran the incident-command staffing cell, redeploying nurses across service lines on rotating twelve-hour cycles. I chair the throughput steering committee, which brings together case management, environmental services, the hospitalist group, and the ED to attack discharge-before-noon and avoidable-day metrics. I also serve as the operational lead for our Epic optimization workstream covering nursing documentation and bed-management modules.

Selected Accomplishments
Led a length-of-stay reduction program across the medical-surgical units that reduced average LOS on those units over an eighteen-month window, primarily by restructuring multidisciplinary rounds, instituting a geographic-rounding model so hospitalists were assigned by unit rather than scattered across the building, and building an electronic discharge-barrier tracker that case managers updated in real time. Reduced reliance on agency and travel nursing by rebuilding the internal float pool, introducing self-scheduling with guardrails, and renegotiating the differential structure for weekend and night shifts. Improved the discharge-before-noon rate substantially on the units I owned by aligning pharmacy, transport, and physician rounding sequence. Co-led the Joint Commission survey readiness effort for two consecutive triennial surveys, owning the tracer program for medication management and nursing care of the patient; both surveys closed with no condition-level findings on my units. Partnered with the quality department to drive down CLABSI and CAUTI rates on the step-down unit through a nurse-driven removal protocol and a central-line maintenance bundle audit.

Prior Role
Saint Brendan Community Hospital — Nurse Manager, Medical-Surgical and Telemetry (2014 to 2019)
Managed a combined medical-surgical and telemetry unit of forty-four beds with approximately ninety direct reports across all shifts. Owned the unit budget, staffing matrix, quality scorecard, and patient-experience results. Brought the unit from chronic understaffing and heavy agency dependence to a stable core staff through a focused recruitment and retention push, a preceptor program for new graduates, and a shared-governance council that gave bedside nurses a real voice in scheduling and practice decisions. Improved HCAHPS nurse-communication scores and reduced first-year turnover among new-graduate nurses. Served as house supervisor on a rotating basis, which gave me whole-hospital operational exposure on nights and weekends.

Early Career
Started as a bedside medical-surgical nurse and progressed to charge nurse and then assistant nurse manager over six years before my first manager role. That bedside foundation is the reason I am credible with frontline staff: I have worked the floor short, I have floated to unfamiliar units, and I have run codes. I do not ask staff to do anything I have not done myself.

Selected Skills and Domains
Inpatient throughput and capacity management; nursing staffing models and labor productivity (worked-hours-per-patient-day); surge and incident-command operations; Joint Commission and CMS regulatory readiness; Epic nursing and bed-management optimization; length-of-stay and avoidable-day reduction; agency and travel-labor management; shared governance and nurse retention; quality improvement methodology including Plan-Do-Study-Act cycles and basic statistical process control; budget ownership at the unit and division level.

Education and Credentials
Master of Science in Nursing, Healthcare Administration concentration. Bachelor of Science in Nursing. Registered Nurse, active and unencumbered. Certified in Nursing Administration. Green Belt training in Lean for healthcare. Member of the regional nurse-executive association.

What I Am Looking For
A senior operations role where I can own throughput and capacity for a larger footprint, ideally at the division or service-line level, in an organization that takes both the financial and the human side of staffing seriously. I am most effective in environments that want an operator who can stand at the bedside and in the boardroom in the same afternoon, and who will tell leadership uncomfortable truths about what staffing levels actually make safe care possible. I am deliberately selective about my next move because I have learned that the operating model an organization actually rewards matters far more than the title on the door.
jd: Mercy-adjacent health system seeks a Senior Clinical Operations Manager to own inpatient throughput, nursing labor productivity, and regulatory readiness for a 500-bed acute-care campus. Reporting to the VP of Patient Care Services, you will be accountable for length-of-stay reduction, avoidable-day elimination, agency-labor spend reduction, and worked-hours-per-patient-day targets across eight inpatient units. You will lead the patient-flow command center, chair the throughput steering committee, and serve as an operational owner for Joint Commission and CMS survey readiness. The role requires demonstrated, quantified results in LOS reduction and agency-spend reduction, fluency in nursing productivity metrics, experience with Epic bed-management and documentation modules, and the judgment to balance financial targets against patient-safety and staff-wellbeing constraints. This is a high-accountability role; the campus has missed its LOS and agency-spend targets for three consecutive quarters, and the board is watching.

history.q: Walk me through your current scope at Mercy Regional — units, beds, direct reports, and the metrics you personally own.
Your resume says you 'reduced average LOS over an eighteen-month window.' Give me the starting number, the ending number, and the unit.
Of that LOS improvement, how much do you attribute to geographic rounding versus the discharge-barrier tracker versus other factors?
Let's talk agency labor. What was your agency and travel spend before and after the float-pool rebuild, in dollars or as a percent of nursing labor?
Walk me through the differential renegotiation. Who did you negotiate with, and what changed in the structure?
You chaired the throughput steering committee. Tell me about one decision the committee made that you personally disagreed with at first.
On the discharge-before-noon improvement — what was the rate when you started and where did it land?
Tell me about a time a staffing decision you made turned out to be wrong, and how you found out.
The CLABSI and CAUTI work — were you the operational owner or was that quality-department-led? Be specific about your role.
This campus has missed LOS and agency targets three quarters running. In your first ninety days, what would you actually do differently from what they're doing now?
I want to push on the LOS number again, because you've given me a range twice but not a hard figure. What was the exact starting LOS in days on the med-surg units?
Help me understand why you can't give me the specific number on a metric you say you owned for eighteen months.

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, I think this line of questioning is missing the point. You keep fishing for one number like that's what good operations looks like. I owned the program, the program worked, the CNO was thrilled, and frankly if you'd ever actually run an inpatient division during a pandemic you'd know LOS moves around month to month and quoting a single decimal is meaningless. I'm not going to pretend I memorized a dashboard to satisfy a checklist. The results were real and my reference will tell you that.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: quoting a single decimal is meaningless

expected-yield: a defused, specific commitment to the actual starting and ending LOS in days on the named med-surg units — or an explicit admission the candidate does not have the figure — rather than the hostile deflection that a single number is meaningless
